expectations.text: Questions are clearly delineated with one concept per question, not walls of text
expectations.evidence: All 20 questions in the transcript are short (1-3 sentences each), clearly formatted with **QUESTION:** prefix, and each targets a single concept. Examples: 'What are you using right now to track reading, if anything?' (one concept: current tools), 'Audiobooks? Or strictly physical/ebook?' (one concept: format support). No question bundles multiple unrelated topics.

expectations.text: At least 2 questions surface topics the initial prompt did not mention
expectations.evidence: The initial prompt is not shown in the transcript, but based on the interview being about a 'reading habit tracker,' several questions surface topics unlikely to have been in the prompt: (1) 'What happens when you miss a day? Some apps use streak-freezes or grace periods' — streak mechanics and psychology of failure. (2) 'Audiobooks? Or strictly physical/ebook?' — format diversity and how metrics shift. (3) 'What about re-reads?' — edge case for data modeling. (4) 'Do you want any kind of reminders or nudges?' — notification strategy. These go well beyond basic reading tracking.

expectations.text: Later questions reference or build on earlier answers
expectations.evidence: Multiple examples: (1) Round 4 asks 'You mentioned streaks and goals' directly referencing the user's Round 1 answer about wanting to understand patterns and stay honest. (2) 'What does "pace" mean to you?' in Round 3 builds on the user's Round 1 mention of wanting to know if they're on track. (3) Round 6 question 'You mentioned SvelteKit earlier in your stack preferences' references prior context. (4) 'You mentioned notes. How important is that?' follows up on the user's earlier Apple Notes comment.

expectations.text: At least 1 question challenges an assumption or pushes back on the idea
expectations.evidence: The interview is thorough but largely accommodating. No question challenges whether the app is needed, whether building a custom tool is worthwhile vs. configuring an existing one differently, whether tracking itself might become counterproductive, or whether the user's goal of 30 books/year is the right target. The closest is 'If you could only build three features for V1, what are they?' which forces prioritization but doesn't challenge an assumption. The question about native vs. web app is a clarification, not a pushback. The interviewer never says anything like 'Have you considered that X might not work because Y?'

expectations.text: The interview explicitly addresses what is NOT in scope
expectations.evidence: Scope boundaries are addressed in multiple places: (1) Auth is explicitly deferred — 'Single-user to start. I can add auth later.' (2) Kindle sync is called 'a future thing.' (3) Goodreads import is labeled 'V1 nice-to-have' not core. (4) Social features are implicitly out — the user says 'I don't care about reviews or what my friends are reading.' (5) The spec codifies this with 'Post-V1 / Future' section listing user accounts, Kindle sync, social features, recommendations, and tags as out of scope.

expectations.text: Final spec has clear sections including problem, scope, and success criteria
expectations.evidence: The spec (spec.md) has well-defined sections: 'Problem Statement' (why Goodreads falls short), 'Product Vision' (one-sentence scope), 'V1 Features (Priority Order)' with 6 numbered features, 'V1 Nice-to-Have' (soft scope boundary), 'Post-V1 / Future' (out of scope), and 'Success Criteria' (4 concrete criteria anchored to 'six months after launch').

expectations.text: Key decisions are documented with rationale, not just listed
expectations.evidence: Several decisions include rationale: (1) 'Streak counter with configurable grace' — rationale given in interview: 'Hard reset feels punishing and makes me want to stop using the app.' (2) UX Principles section explains the 'why' behind each principle: '15-second logging. If it takes longer, people stop using it.' (3) 'Catalog is secondary. This isn't a book database. It's a habit tracker that happens to involve books.' (4) Derived metrics are 'computed, not stored' — a deliberate data modeling decision. (5) Auth decision rationale: 'None for V1 (single-user, private deployment). Add auth when opening to multiple users.' However, some decisions lack rationale — e.g., why PostgreSQL over something lighter for a single-user app, why Open Library over Google Books.

expectations.text: Spec identifies risks or open questions that remain unresolved
expectations.evidence: The spec does not contain a 'Risks,' 'Open Questions,' or 'Unknowns' section. Several unresolved questions exist but aren't called out: (1) How reliable is Open Library API for metadata coverage? What happens when a book isn't found? (2) How will Goodreads CSV import handle format mismatches? (3) PWA limitations on iOS (no push notifications) conflict with the daily reminder feature. (4) How will grace days interact with the streak algorithm at week boundaries? (5) No mention of data backup/export strategy for a self-hosted single-user app. The spec reads as fully decided, which is itself a risk.

claims.claim: 20 questions were asked across 7 rounds plus final clarifications
claims.evidence: Counted 20 QUESTION entries across Rounds 1-7 and Final Clarifications in the transcript

claims.claim: The spec covers a complete data model with Books, Sessions, and Goals tables
claims.evidence: spec.md contains three tables (Books with 13 fields, Reading Sessions with 11 fields, Goals with 5 fields) plus derived metrics

claims.claim: Session logging can be done in under 15 seconds
claims.evidence: This is a design aspiration stated by the user and adopted as UX Principle #1, but no prototyping or interaction design validates the claim. The logging form includes book selection, duration entry, page/percentage entry, and optional note — achieving this in 15 seconds depends entirely on implementation.

claims.claim: The interview covered edge cases like audiobooks, re-reads, and concurrent books
claims.evidence: Round 7 explicitly covers all three: audiobooks (pages vs time/percentage), re-reads ('treat it as a new read, separate entry'), and multiple concurrent books ('usually two — one fiction, one nonfiction').

suggestions.assertion: At least 2 questions surface topics the initial prompt did not mention
suggestions.reason: Without the original prompt in the transcript, the grader must guess what was and wasn't mentioned. Including the original prompt as part of the transcript would make this assertion verifiable rather than inferential.

No assertion checks interview pacing or depth balance. This interview spent 7 rounds and 20 questions on a relatively straightforward app — there's no check for whether the interview went too long or too shallow in any area. A 'no round has fewer than 2 questions' or 'interview stays under N rounds' assertion could catch lopsided coverage.

No assertion checks whether the spec is traceable back to the interview — i.e., that every spec feature maps to something the user actually said. The spec includes 'dark mode' as a nice-to-have, but the user never mentioned it. This could be a good addition or scope creep depending on perspective, and no assertion catches it.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions cover question quality, interview dynamics, and spec structure well. The main gaps are: (1) the original prompt isn't available for comparison, weakening assertion #2, and (2) no assertion checks spec-to-interview traceability, which would catch features that were invented rather than elicited.
